findings.title: eval() executed on metadata string loaded from .npz file (arbitrary code execution)
findings.evidence: load_residuals does `metadata_str = str(data['metadata'].item())` then `metadata = eval(metadata_str)` with the in-code comment `# Use eval carefully! Assumes trusted source.`. The metadata is the stringified dict written by save_residuals (line 148, `np.array([str(meta_str_dict)])`). Any .npz file fed to load_residuals can carry an arbitrary Python expression in its 'metadata' array that eval will execute, giving code execution from a data file.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (4) 'generate synthetic observations ... for parameter recovery testing' and the load/save round-trip of residual data — loading a results artifact must not execute untrusted code.
findings.falsifier_note: residual_analysis.py:215 is `metadata = eval(metadata_str)` with comment 'Use eval carefully! Assumes trusted source.', where metadata_str derives from data['metadata'].item() (line 214) loaded from the .npz, and save_residuals writes it as a stringified dict at line 148. A crafted .npz metadata array's string is eval'd, giving arbitrary code execution from a data file. Confirmed at the anchor.

findings.title: np.load called with allow_pickle=True on user-supplied path (unsafe deserialization)
findings.evidence: `data = np.load(filepath, allow_pickle=True)` in load_residuals; filepath is an arbitrary caller/ensemble-supplied path. allow_pickle=True permits a crafted .npz to deserialize arbitrary Python objects (pickle) on load, a known RCE vector, compounding the eval() issue on the same path.
findings.intent_link: Violates the safe load/save residual round-trip required by intent (2) (compute orbital residuals) — reading a stored artifact should be inert.
findings.falsifier_note: residual_analysis.py:206 is `data = np.load(filepath, allow_pickle=True)` with comment 'Allow pickle for metadata potentially', on a caller-supplied filepath. allow_pickle=True permits pickle-based object deserialization, a known RCE vector, compounding F001 on the same load path. Confirmed at the anchor.

findings.title: km/s -> AU/day conversion constant is wrong by ~86x
findings.evidence: `KM_S_TO_AU_DAY = 1.0 / 1.731e6 * 86400.0` evaluates to ~0.0499. Correct value: 1 km/s = 86400 s/day / 1.496e8 km/AU = 5.78e-4 AU/day. The comment itself states '1 AU = 1.496e8 km' but the code uses 1.731e6 in the denominator, yielding velocities ~86x too large. sample_velocity (lines 65-69) multiplies all sampled component speeds by this constant, so every sampled PBH velocity is mis-scaled.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (3) 'run large Monte Carlo ensembles to estimate detection rates' — encounter velocities feed directly into the impulse kick (Δv ∝ 1/v_rel), so detection-rate estimates are systematically corrupted.
findings.falsifier_note: parameter_sampler.py:11 is `KM_S_TO_AU_DAY = 1.0 / 1.731e6 * 86400.0` = 0.0499. Correct: 1 km/s = 86400/1.496e8 = 5.78e-4 AU/day, so the constant is ~86x too large. sample_velocity (lines 65-67) multiplies every sampled component by this factor. The comment itself cites 1.496e8 km/AU yet the code uses 1.731e6. Confirmed.

findings.title: PBH mass key mismatch: simulation reads pbh_params['mass'] but sampler produces 'mass_msun' (KeyError aborts every perturbed run)
findings.evidence: run_single_simulation does `pbh_mass = pbh_params['mass']`, but parameter_sampler.generate_pbh_sample (parameter_sampler.py:112-117) emits keys 'mass_msun','impact_param_au','velocity_au_day','t_encounter_years'. ensemble_runner passes pbh_samples[i] straight through (ensemble_runner.py:72, run_parallel_simulations -> run_single_simulation). `pbh_params['mass']` therefore raises KeyError, caught by the broad except at simulation_runner.py:180 returning (None,None,None); ensemble_runner.py:75 then raises RuntimeError. The end-to-end ensemble can never produce a perturbed result.
findings.intent_link: Directly breaks intent (1) and (3): N-body PBH-perturbed runs and Monte-Carlo detection ensembles cannot complete a single perturbed member.
findings.falsifier_note: simulation_runner.py:84 reads `pbh_mass = pbh_params['mass']`; generate_pbh_sample (parameter_sampler.py:112-117) emits 'mass_msun' not 'mass'. ensemble_runner.py:220 passes pbh_samples[i] verbatim through run_parallel_simulations (line 72) to run_single_simulation, so line 84 raises KeyError, caught by the broad except at simulation_runner.py:180-184 returning (None,None,None); ensemble_runner.py:75-76 then raises RuntimeError. End-to-end ensemble cannot produce a perturbed result. Confirmed.

findings.title: Impact parameter key mismatch: 'impact_param' default silently discards sampled 'impact_param_au'
findings.evidence: `pbh_initial_pos = np.array([-1000.0, pbh_params.get('impact_param', 100.0), 0.0])`. The sampler produces 'impact_param_au' (parameter_sampler.py:114), never 'impact_param', so .get always returns the hardcoded default 100.0 AU. Every PBH is placed at the same y-offset regardless of the sampled impact parameter.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (3): detection rate as a function of PBH parameters is meaningless if the sampled impact parameter never reaches the simulation.
findings.falsifier_note: simulation_runner.py:91 is `pbh_initial_pos = np.array([-1000.0, pbh_params.get('impact_param', 100.0), 0.0])`. The sampler emits 'impact_param_au' (parameter_sampler.py:114), never 'impact_param', so .get always returns the hardcoded 100.0. (Note: in the ensemble path execution never reaches line 91 because line 84 KeyErrors first, but the key-mismatch defect at the cited anchor is real.) Confirmed.

findings.title: Hardcoded kick target 'body_3' does not exist for the configured body set
findings.evidence: `target_body_label = "body_3"` is hardcoded, but labels are built as `body_{i}` with body_0 renamed 'Sun' (simulation_runner.py:48-50). For the example/ensemble setup with masses of length 3 (Sun, Body1, Body2) the labels are ['Sun','body_1','body_2'] — there is no 'body_3'. apply_analytic_kick then fails to find the target (n_body_simulation.py:281-285) and returns False, so no kick is applied.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (1)/(2): the PBH impulse is never applied to the intended body, so residuals reflect no perturbation.
findings.falsifier_note: simulation_runner.py:104 hardcodes `target_body_label = "body_3"`, while labels are built as f"body_{i}" with index 0 renamed 'Sun' (lines 48-50). For the 3-body example/ensemble setup labels are ['Sun','body_1','body_2'] — no 'body_3'. apply_analytic_kick cannot find the target and returns False (n_body_simulation.py:281-285), so no kick is applied. Confirmed at the anchor.

findings.title: run_ensemble has no return statement; returns None while caller treats it as a list
findings.evidence: run_ensemble's docstring states 'Returns: list: A list of summary dictionaries', but the function body ends at the print on ensemble_runner.py:277 with no `return`, so it returns None. The __main__ block (ensemble_runner.py:460) assigns `ensemble_results = run_ensemble(...)` then iterates `sum(1 for r in ensemble_results ...)` at line 472, which raises TypeError ('NoneType' is not iterable).
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (3): the ensemble driver cannot hand its results to detection-rate analysis; documented return contract is broken.
findings.falsifier_note: run_ensemble (ensemble_runner.py:144) docstring (line 172) promises 'list of summary dictionaries', but the body ends at the print on line 277 with no return statement (line 282 begins a new function), so it returns None. The __main__ block assigns `ensemble_results = run_ensemble(...)` at line 460 and iterates `sum(1 for r in ensemble_results ...)` at line 472, raising TypeError on NoneType. Confirmed.

findings.title: Absolute import 'from analytic_impulse import ...' breaks when n_body_simulation is imported as a package module
findings.evidence: n_body_simulation.py uses `from analytic_impulse import calculate_velocity_kick, G as analytic_G` (top-level import), while simulation_runner.py:4 and ensemble_runner.py:10-13 import it via package-relative `from . import n_body_simulation`. Under package execution (e.g. `python -m src.ensemble_runner`) the top-level name 'analytic_impulse' is not on sys.path, so importing n_body_simulation raises ModuleNotFoundError unless src happens to be on the path.
findings.intent_link: Violates the 'complete, modular computational pipeline' intent — modules cannot be reliably imported together as a package.
findings.falsifier_note: n_body_simulation.py:4 uses the top-level absolute import `from analytic_impulse import calculate_velocity_kick, G as analytic_G`, while simulation_runner.py:4 and ensemble_runner.py:10-13 import it package-relatively (from . import n_body_simulation). Under `python -m src.ensemble_runner` the project root (not src/) is on sys.path, so the bare name 'analytic_impulse' (located in src/) is unresolved and importing n_body_simulation raises ModuleNotFoundError. Confirmed real path-fragility at the anchor.

findings.title: add_solar_system swallows exceptions and continues with a possibly empty simulation
findings.evidence: The `try/except Exception as e:` around `self.sim.add_solar_system(date=date)` only does `print(f"Error adding Solar System bodies: {e}")` (lines 97-99) and neither re-raises nor returns a status. A failed JPL Horizons fetch leaves the simulation with no/partial bodies, and downstream code proceeds as if bodies were added. Comment 'Consider fallback or raising the error' confirms no handling.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (1): a baseline Solar System must exist for residual computation; silently continuing produces invalid baselines.
findings.falsifier_note: n_body_simulation.py:97-99 confirms `except Exception as e:` only `print(f"Error adding Solar System bodies: {e}")` with comment 'Consider fallback or raising the error' (line 99). No re-raise, no status return; the method continues and downstream code proceeds regardless of whether bodies were added.

findings.title: PBH initial position is a placeholder; t_encounter and proper geometry are ignored
findings.evidence: Code comments admit the stub: `# Placeholder: Use a dummy position far away initially` and `# TODO: Calculate initial PBH position based on encounter parameters relative to target`. pbh_initial_pos is fixed at [-1000, <default 100>, 0] and the sampled 't_encounter_years' (parameter_sampler.py:116) is never used to place the PBH; apply_analytic_kick computes t_ca purely from this placeholder geometry (n_body_simulation.py:234-244).
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (1) 'N-body simulations of PBH encounters using impulse-approximation shortcuts' and the stated Tran et al. methodology — encounter timing/geometry is not actually realized.
findings.falsifier_note: simulation_runner.py:89-91 contains the literal placeholder comments and sets `pbh_initial_pos = np.array([-1000.0, pbh_params.get('impact_param', 100.0), 0.0])` with '# Needs proper calculation!'. The sampler's 't_encounter_years' (parameter_sampler.py:116) is never read in this path; apply_analytic_kick computes t_ca purely from the placed-geometry state (n_body_simulation.py:234-244).

findings.title: Velocity kick points away from the PBH (sign error in kick direction)
findings.evidence: r_ca is the relative position at closest approach computed from `initial_relative_position = pbh_position - body_position` (line 33) and `r_ca = initial_relative_position + relative_velocity * t_ca` (line 48), i.e. it points from the body toward the PBH. Gravity should accelerate the body toward the PBH (along +r_ca), but `kick_direction = -r_ca / b` (line 66) points away from the PBH, so the impulse is applied in the wrong direction.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (1)/(2): the impulse-approximation kick must attract the body toward the PBH path; an inverted kick yields physically wrong residuals.
findings.falsifier_note: analytic_impulse.py:33 `initial_relative_position = pbh_position - body_position` and line 48 `r_ca = initial_relative_position + relative_velocity * t_ca` both point from body toward PBH. Gravity accelerates the body toward the PBH (+r_ca), but line 66 sets `kick_direction = -r_ca / b` (away from PBH). apply_kick adds delta_v directly (analytic_impulse.py:95 `current_velocity + delta_v`) with no downstream negation, so the wrong-direction impulse is applied as-is.

findings.title: JSON serialization of numpy values in summaries/results fails, breaking checkpointing and final output
findings.evidence: summary['pbh_params'] is the sampler dict containing a numpy array ('velocity_au_day') and numpy float ('mass_msun') (parameter_sampler.py:112-117). `json.dump(summary, f_sum, indent=4)` (ensemble_runner.py:136), the checkpoint dump (line 249) and the final dump (line 263) all serialize these objects; the standard json encoder cannot serialize numpy ndarray/float64 and raises TypeError. These are caught only by surrounding try/except that print warnings, so per-member summaries, checkpoints, and final aggregated results are not persisted.
findings.intent_link: Violates intent (3): ensemble results and checkpoints (needed to compute detection rates and resume long runs) are silently not saved.
findings.falsifier_note: summary['pbh_params'] is the raw sampler dict (ensemble_runner.py:54) containing numpy ndarray 'velocity_au_day' and numpy float 'mass_msun' (parameter_sampler.py:113,115). json.dump at ensemble_runner.py:136 (per-member), :249 (checkpoint), and :263 (final) cannot serialize numpy types and raises TypeError; each is wrapped in try/except that only prints a warning (lines 137-138, 251-252, 273-274), so these outputs are not persisted.

findings.title: Particle-count resize in the integration loop is lossy and uses inconsistent initial allocation
findings.evidence: After the PBH branch reallocates arrays to num_particles+1 (lines 126-127), the main loop still guards with `if len(current_state) != positions_out.shape[1]:` and reallocates/copies arrays mid-loop (lines 151-161), explicitly noted 'this is inefficient, better to size correctly initially'. The min-shape copy (lines 158-161) silently drops data if shapes shrink and the per-particle bounds check (line 165-169) drops out-of-bounds particles, masking state-array sizing errors instead of failing.
findings.intent_link: Weakens intent (1)/(2): reliable per-step state capture is required to compute correct residuals.
findings.falsifier_note: simulation_runner.py:125-127 allocates arrays to len(masses)+1, yet the loop at lines 151-161 re-checks particle count and reallocates/copies with the comment 'this is inefficient, better to size correctly initially'. The min-shape copy (158-161) drops data on shrink and the bounds check (165-169) silently skips out-of-range particles, masking sizing errors rather than failing.

findings.title: Stub example advertised as runnable single-flyby demo performs no simulation
findings.evidence: The module docstring claims it 'shows how to: 1. Set up a solar system model ... 3. Run the simulation 4. Analyze the results', but the body only prints parameters and then `print("\nThis is a placeholder example. The actual implementation will:")` — it never imports or calls any src module to run a flyby.
findings.intent_link: Mismatch with intent's 'one stub example' / end-to-end usage expectation: the only example does not exercise the pipeline.
findings.falsifier_note: examples/single_flyby_example.py docstring (lines 4-8) advertises set-up/run/analyze, but the body (lines 20-39) only prints parameters then `print("\nThis is a placeholder example. The actual implementation will:")` (line 34). It imports no src module and runs no simulation.

findings.title: run_ensemble() has no return statement — always returns None
findings.evidence: The function body ends at line 277 with `print(f"\nEnsemble run finished. {len(results)} members processed.")` and no `return results` statement. The docstring at line 173 states 'Returns: list: A list of summary dictionaries, one for each completed member.' All callers receive None. In the __main__ block at line 460, `ensemble_results = run_ensemble(...)` assigns None, so the immediately following `sum(1 for r in ensemble_results if ...)` at line 472 raises TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable.
findings.intent_link: Intent (3): run large Monte Carlo ensembles to estimate detection rates. The ensemble runner is the top-level orchestrator; returning None instead of results silently destroys all computed outputs.
findings.falsifier_note: run_ensemble ends at ensemble_runner.py:277 with a print and no `return results`, despite the docstring promising a list of summary dicts. At line 460 `ensemble_results = run_ensemble(...)` receives None, and line 472 `sum(1 for r in ensemble_results if r.get('status') == 'completed')` iterates None → TypeError.

findings.title: PBH parameter key mismatch between parameter_sampler and simulation_runner causes KeyError in every perturbed run
findings.evidence: parameter_sampler.py:112 produces dicts with key 'mass_msun'. simulation_runner.py:84 accesses `pbh_params['mass']` — missing key raises KeyError. Additionally simulation_runner.py:91 calls `pbh_params.get('impact_param', 100.0)` while parameter_sampler.py:114 uses key 'impact_param_au'. ensemble_runner.py:72 passes the sampler output directly to run_parallel_simulations with no key mapping, so every perturbed ensemble member crashes before any simulation runs.
findings.intent_link: Intent (1) and (3): N-body simulations of PBH encounters and Monte Carlo ensembles are completely non-functional for any call path that goes parameter_sampler → ensemble_runner → simulation_runner.
findings.falsifier_note: parameter_sampler.py:113 emits key 'mass_msun' but simulation_runner.py:84 reads `pbh_params['mass']` → KeyError; simulation_runner.py:91 uses `.get('impact_param', 100.0)` while the sampler key is 'impact_param_au' (parameter_sampler.py:114). ensemble_runner.py:72 passes the sampler dict straight through with no key mapping, so every perturbed member crashes at line 84 (caught by the try/except at line 123).

findings.title: KM_S_TO_AU_DAY conversion constant is wrong by a factor of ~86
findings.evidence: Line 11: `KM_S_TO_AU_DAY = 1.0 / 1.731e6 * 86400.0` evaluates to ≈0.04991 AU/day per km/s. The correct conversion using 1 AU = 1.496e8 km, 1 day = 86400 s is 86400/1.496e8 ≈ 5.775e-4 AU/day per km/s. The code's divisor (1.731e6) is ~86× smaller than the correct value (1.496e8), making every sampled PBH velocity ~86× too large. At the default sigma_v=200 km/s, the 1-sigma speed becomes ~9.98 AU/day (≈1.73×10⁴ km/s, about 6% of c) instead of the physically expected ~0.115 AU/day (~200 km/s).
findings.intent_link: Intent (3): Monte Carlo detection rate estimates depend on physically realistic velocity sampling. Velocities 86× too large invalidate all sampled encounter geometries and resulting residual statistics.
findings.falsifier_note: src/parameter_sampler.py:11 reads `KM_S_TO_AU_DAY = 1.0 / 1.731e6 * 86400.0` = 86400/1.731e6 ≈ 0.0499 AU/day per km/s. Correct value is 86400/1.496e8 ≈ 5.78e-4. The divisor 1.731e6 is ~86× smaller than 1.496e8 (the AU-in-km figure even named in the same line's comment), so sampled velocities are ~86× too large. Confirmed.

findings.title: Analytic impulse example PBH velocity is ~820× too large — comment and hardcoded value both wrong
findings.evidence: Lines 104-105: comment says '200 km/s -> 94.8 AU/day -> … ~= 5510 AU/(yr/2pi)'; hardcoded velocity is `[5510.0, 0, 0]`. Correct conversion: 200 km/s × 86400 s/day / 1.496e8 km/AU ≈ 0.1155 AU/day = 0.1155 × VELOCITY_DAY_TO_REBOUND ≈ 6.71 AU/(yr/2π). The hardcoded 5510 AU/(yr/2π) corresponds to ≈94.8 AU/day ≈ 164,000 km/s ≈ 0.55c — highly relativistic, making the impulse approximation (which assumes v_rel ≪ c) physically invalid for this example.
findings.intent_link: Intent (2): impulse-approximation shortcuts require physically realistic relative velocities; using 0.55c violates the non-relativistic assumption of the analytic formula.
findings.falsifier_note: src/analytic_impulse.py:104-105: comment claims '200 km/s -> 94.8 AU/day ... ~= 5510 AU/(yr/2pi)' and hardcodes `pbh_velocity_example = np.array([5510.0, 0, 0])`. Correct: 200 km/s = 200*86400/1.496e8 ≈ 0.1155 AU/day, not 94.8 (~820× off); 5510 = 94.8 * 365.25/(2π). Both comment and value are wrong/relativistic. Confirmed.

findings.title: n_body_simulation.py uses bare absolute import of analytic_impulse — fails when imported as package
findings.evidence: Line 4: `from analytic_impulse import calculate_velocity_kick, G as analytic_G` — unconditional absolute import with no try/except fallback. In Python 3, this resolves as a top-level module lookup. When the module is imported via `from src.n_body_simulation import NBodySimulation` (as in tests/test_n_body_simulation.py:13), the project root is in sys.path but src/ is not, so `analytic_impulse` is not findable and raises ModuleNotFoundError. Other modules (simulation_runner.py:4-18, ensemble_runner.py:10-19, synthetic_data.py:6-9) all use try/except relative-then-bare import patterns to handle this; n_body_simulation.py does not.
findings.intent_link: Intent (1): the NBodySimulation class is the core simulation engine; an import failure at module load time blocks all N-body simulation.
findings.falsifier_note: src/n_body_simulation.py:4 has unconditional `from analytic_impulse import calculate_velocity_kick, G as analytic_G` with no try/except. tests/test_n_body_simulation.py:13 imports `from src.n_body_simulation import NBodySimulation`; conftest.py:8 adds only the project root (not src/) to sys.path, so the bare top-level `analytic_impulse` lookup fails -> ModuleNotFoundError. Core claim confirmed. Minor inaccuracy in evidence: simulation_runner.py:4-5 actually uses pure relative imports (`from . import ...`), not try/except; this does not affect the finding's substance.

findings.title: PBH initial position calculation is a placeholder — perturbed simulations never run a physical encounter
findings.evidence: Lines 88-91: `# TODO: Calculate initial PBH position based on encounter parameters relative to target` / `pbh_initial_pos = np.array([-1000.0, pbh_params.get('impact_param', 100.0), 0.0]) # Needs proper calculation!`. The y-coordinate is set to the impact parameter scalar (a distance, not a position component). The pseudocode (docs/pseudocode.md:206-228) defines CreatePBHBody() using SphericalToCartesian(r0, theta0, phi0) and VelocityVectorFromAngles(). No such calculation exists; the PBH is always placed at (-1000, b, 0) regardless of encounter geometry.
findings.intent_link: Intent (1): N-body simulations of PBH encounters require a physically computed initial PBH position from encounter parameters (r0, theta0, phi0, alpha, beta per paper arXiv:2312.17217v3 Section III).
findings.falsifier_note: src/simulation_runner.py:88-91 confirmed: TODO comment plus `pbh_initial_pos = np.array([-1000.0, pbh_params.get('impact_param', 100.0), 0.0]) # Needs proper calculation!`. The y-coordinate is set to the impact-parameter scalar regardless of encounter geometry; no spherical-to-cartesian PBH placement exists. Confirmed.

findings.title: Target body for analytic kick hardcoded as 'body_3' — only works for a specific 4-body setup
findings.evidence: Line 104: `target_body_label = "body_3" # Assuming Earth is body 3 after Sun, Merc, Venus`. Labels are assigned at lines 48-50 as `[f"body_{i}" for i in range(len(masses))]`. If fewer than 4 bodies are used (e.g., the 3-body example in __main__), 'body_3' does not exist and apply_analytic_kick() returns False silently. No mechanism is provided to specify the target body via pbh_params or arguments.
findings.intent_link: Intent (1): the Solar System simulation should apply kicks to the correct bodies. Hardcoding 'body_3' prevents generalization to arbitrary Solar System configurations.
findings.falsifier_note: src/simulation_runner.py:104 `target_body_label = "body_3"` with comment assuming Earth is body 3. Labels generated at line 48 as `[f"body_{i}" for i in range(len(masses))]`, so with <4 bodies 'body_3' is absent and the kick silently fails. No argument/param to override the target. Confirmed.

findings.title: q_fom figure-of-merit (paper Eq. 17) is entirely unimplemented — core detection metric missing
findings.evidence: Lines 233-240 show commented-out stubs only: `# def calculate_observables(positions, velocities): / #     pass` and `# def calculate_q_fom(residuals_observables): / #     pass`. README.md:53 Feature #4 states 'Evaluate figure-of-merit q_fom for detection'. docs/pseudocode.md:315-338 provides a complete ComputeQFOM() specification based on paper Eq. (17). ensemble_runner.py is_detected() and calculate_detection_rates() use peak position residual as a crude proxy threshold rather than q_fom. The actual detection criterion from Tran et al. is absent.
findings.intent_link: Intent (3): Monte Carlo detection rate estimation requires q_fom (Eq. 17 of arXiv:2312.17217v3) as the detection statistic; the substitute threshold on raw peak AU residual is not the paper's methodology.
findings.falsifier_note: src/residual_analysis.py:233-240 contains only commented-out stubs for `calculate_observables` and `calculate_q_fom` (both `# ... pass`). No q_fom implementation exists; the figure-of-merit detection metric is unimplemented. Confirmed.

findings.title: README project structure lists wrong module names not present in the repository
findings.evidence: README.md:128-131 shows: `src/nbody.py`, `src/pbhsampler.py`, `src/residuals.py`, `src/parameter_recovery.py`. Actual source files are `src/n_body_simulation.py`, `src/parameter_sampler.py`, `src/residual_analysis.py`. No `parameter_recovery.py` exists. README also shows `scripts/single_flyby.py`, `scripts/ensemble_flyby.py`, `scripts/param_recovery.py` (lines 134-137) and `examples/SingleFlyby.ipynb` (line 139) — none of these exist in the repository.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to be a usable framework for researchers; a README directing users to non-existent files and scripts defeats first-contact usability.
findings.falsifier_note: README.md:128-131 lists `src/nbody.py`, `src/pbhsampler.py`, `src/residuals.py`, `src/parameter_recovery.py`; actual files are n_body_simulation.py, parameter_sampler.py, residual_analysis.py and there is no parameter_recovery.py. Lines 134-139 list scripts/ files and examples/SingleFlyby.ipynb that do not exist in the audit surface. Confirmed.

findings.title: README Usage section references non-existent CLI scripts with flags that match no implemented interface
findings.evidence: README.md:98-113 specifies three commands: `python scripts/single_flyby.py --mass 1e-9 --r0 450 --alpha 0.004 --beta 3.1415`, `python scripts/ensemble_flyby.py --n 100000 --mass-base 1e-6`, `python scripts/param_recovery.py --input residual_data.npz`. No `scripts/` directory exists; the only runnable example is `examples/single_flyby_example.py` which itself is a stub (line 34: 'This is a placeholder example'). The flags `--r0`, `--alpha`, `--beta` correspond to encounter-geometry parameters that are not implemented in any module.
findings.intent_link: The project claims to enable researchers to run simulations; the documented interface is entirely fictional.
findings.falsifier_note: README.md:98-113 documents `scripts/single_flyby.py --mass ... --r0 ... --alpha ... --beta ...`, `scripts/ensemble_flyby.py`, `scripts/param_recovery.py`. No scripts/ directory exists in the audit surface; the only example, examples/single_flyby_example.py, is a stub (line 34: 'This is a placeholder example.'). The --r0/--alpha/--beta geometry flags match no implemented interface. Confirmed.

findings.title: examples/single_flyby_example.py is a non-functional stub — prints placeholder text instead of running simulation
findings.evidence: Line 34: `print("\nThis is a placeholder example. The actual implementation will:")` — the entire function body consists of `print()` calls describing what will be done, with no imports of or calls to any simulation module (n_body_simulation, simulation_runner, analytic_impulse, etc.). README.md:115 directs users to the examples/ directory for 'a guided walkthrough'.
findings.intent_link: Intent (1): the example is the primary user-facing demonstration of running an N-body PBH flyby simulation.
findings.falsifier_note: examples/single_flyby_example.py:34 prints the placeholder text verbatim; the entire run_single_flyby_example() body (lines 18-39) is only print() calls and parameter literals, with no import of or call to any src/ simulation module. README.md:115 directs users to examples/ for a guided walkthrough. Confirmed stub.

findings.title: eval() used to deserialize untrusted metadata from .npz files — code injection risk
findings.evidence: Line 215: `metadata = eval(metadata_str) # Use eval carefully! Assumes trusted source.` The metadata string was stored as `str({...})` at save_residuals line 148 and is loaded back with `allow_pickle=True` at line 206. eval() on a crafted .npz file's metadata field executes arbitrary Python. The comment acknowledges the risk but provides no safe alternative (e.g., ast.literal_eval or json.loads).
findings.intent_link: Intent (4): synthetic observations are loaded from .npz files for parameter recovery testing; any external or shared .npz file becomes a code-injection vector.
findings.falsifier_note: src/residual_analysis.py:215 calls eval(metadata_str) with the comment 'Use eval carefully! Assumes trusted source.' The string comes from data['metadata'] loaded via np.load(allow_pickle=True) at line 206 — arbitrary Python executes on a crafted .npz. No ast.literal_eval/json.loads alternative present. Confirmed code-injection risk.

findings.title: residual_analysis.py __main__ block: stats calculation is inside the failure (else) branch — dead code on success
findings.evidence: Lines 363-410: `if res_t is not None and res_p is not None and res_v is not None:` block handles successful computation (save/load). The `else:` block at line 395 prints 'Residual computation failed (returned None).' and then at line 398 calls `calculate_residual_stats(res_p, res_v)` — but at this point res_p and res_v are None since residual computation failed. The stats calculation never runs when residuals are successfully computed; it runs only on failure, where it calls stats on None inputs.
findings.intent_link: Intent (2): the __main__ demonstration of residual statistics is a key validation of the analysis pipeline; the inverted branch means the stats example is unreachable in the success path.
findings.falsifier_note: src/residual_analysis.py: the success branch (line 363 'if res_t is not None...') handles save/load; the else: at line 394 prints 'Residual computation failed'. The 'Calculate Stats' block (lines 397-410, calculate_residual_stats(res_p, res_v)) is indented at 8 spaces inside that else branch, so it runs only on failure — where res_p/res_v are None — and never on success. Confirmed.

findings.title: visualization.py calls plt.show() unconditionally in every plot function — blocks headless batch execution
findings.evidence: plt.show() is called unconditionally at: line 76 (plot_trajectories_2d), line 175 (plot_residual_timeseries), line 314 (plot_detection_scatter), line 384 (plot_binned_detection_rate). In server or batch ensemble contexts (which the ensemble_runner is designed for), plt.show() either raises a display error or blocks execution waiting for GUI interaction. No `show=True` parameter or guard is provided.
findings.intent_link: Intent (3): visualization is expected to operate alongside large ensemble runs; unconditional plt.show() makes the module unusable in the primary production context.
findings.falsifier_note: grep confirms unconditional plt.show() at src/visualization.py:76, 175, 314, 384 (one per plot function), with no show= parameter or headless guard. Blocks/errors in batch/server ensemble contexts. Confirmed.

findings.title: setup.py declares pytest and pytest-cov in install_requires — test tools become mandatory runtime dependencies
findings.evidence: setup.py lines 14-15: `"pytest"`, `"pytest-cov"` appear inside `install_requires=[...]`. Test frameworks are development/CI dependencies; placing them in install_requires forces every `pip install primordial_encounters` (including in production simulation environments) to also install pytest. Correct placement is `extras_require={'dev': ['pytest', 'pytest-cov']}`.
findings.intent_link: Packaging correctness is part of providing a usable research framework.
findings.falsifier_note: setup.py install_requires (lines 10-17) contains 'pytest' (line 15) and 'pytest-cov' (line 16) — minor off-by-one vs cited line 14 but inside install_requires, not extras_require. Test frameworks forced as runtime deps. Confirmed.

findings.title: setup.py find_packages() will not find src/ — package name 'primordial_encounters' matches no directory
findings.evidence: setup.py:8-9: `packages=find_packages()` requires `__init__.py` in each package directory. There is no `src/__init__.py` in the repository surface (only `tests/__init__.py` exists). find_packages() discovers only `tests`. The declared `name='primordial_encounters'` matches no directory; the actual code lives in `src/`. A pip-installed package would contain only the tests module and none of the simulation code.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to be pip-installable as 'primordial_encounters'; the setup.py cannot produce a functioning installation.
findings.falsifier_note: setup.py:9 uses packages=find_packages(); `ls src/__init__.py` returns 'No such file or directory', so src/ is not a discoverable package and only tests/ (which has __init__.py) would be found. name='primordial_encounters' matches no directory. A pip install would omit all simulation code. Confirmed.

findings.title: Parameter recovery feature documented in README but no module exists
findings.evidence: README.md:53-57 Feature #5: 'Parameter Recovery & Statistical Testing — Recover PBH mass and trajectory from synthetic observed orbital perturbations — Perform likelihood ratio tests vs. a no PBH null hypothesis'. docs/pseudocode.md:429-475 provides a complete RecoverPBHParameters() specification. No `parameter_recovery.py` or equivalent exists in src/. synthetic_data.py adds noise to residuals but performs no fitting or hypothesis testing.
findings.intent_link: Intent (4): parameter recovery testing is listed as a primary goal of the project.
findings.falsifier_note: README.md:53-56 documents Feature 5 'Parameter Recovery & Statistical Testing' (recover PBH mass, likelihood ratio tests), and README:110-113 references scripts/param_recovery.py. grep for parameter_recovery|RecoverPBH|likelihood across src/ returns no files; src/ listing shows no parameter_recovery module. synthetic_data.py only adds noise. Confirmed doc-drift.

findings.title: Spectral analysis feature documented in README and pseudocode but no module exists
findings.evidence: README.md:58-61 Feature #6: 'Spectral Analysis (Optional) — Fourier methods to confirm the near-monochromatic nature of orbital deviations'. docs/pseudocode.md:282-308 specifies ComputePowerSpectrum() and AnalyzeResidualSpectrum(). No FFT/spectral analysis code exists in any src/ module.
findings.intent_link: Intent (2): the paper's Figure 3 right panel shows spectral analysis of residuals; replicating this result requires the missing spectral module.
findings.falsifier_note: README.md:58-60 documents Feature 6 'Spectral Analysis (Optional)' with Fourier methods. grep for power_spectrum|spectral|fft|periodogram across src/ returns no files. No FFT/spectral module exists in src/. Confirmed doc-drift.

findings.title: README references MIT LICENSE file that does not exist
findings.evidence: README.md:159: 'This project is offered under the MIT License(LICENSE).' — hyperlink points to a LICENSE file. No LICENSE file appears in the repository surface. The repository has no license declaration, making the legal status of the code ambiguous.
findings.intent_link: A research framework intended for public use requires a valid license file to be legally usable by others.
findings.falsifier_note: README.md:160 contains 'This project is offered under the [MIT License](LICENSE).' linking to a LICENSE file. `ls` of the repo root shows no LICENSE file (grep -i licen returned nothing), and LICENSE is absent from the Stage-1 surface. The dangling link and missing license declaration are confirmed.

findings.title: test_initialization is a bare pass — no physics correctness tests exist anywhere in the test suite
findings.evidence: test_n_body_simulation.py:23-27: `def test_initialization(self): / pass` — decorated with @unittest.skipIf but body is pass regardless. test_project_structure() at line 29 only checks that four directories exist. No test covers: calculate_velocity_kick() accuracy, residual computation correctness, parameter sampling distributions, detection rate calculation, or any numerical physics result. pytest.ini defines testpaths=tests but there are no tests for any src/ module beyond directory-existence checks.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to replicate the methodology of Tran et al.; without tests verifying numerical correctness, any physics bug (like the velocity conversion errors) goes undetected.
findings.falsifier_note: tests/test_n_body_simulation.py:23-27 is `def test_initialization(self): ... pass` (docstring + comment + bare pass). test_project_structure (lines 29-40) only asserts os.path.isdir for src/tests/examples/data. No numerical/physics assertions exist; the only other test file content is directory existence. Confirmed.

findings.title: parameter_sampler.py imports scipy.stats but never calls any scipy function
findings.evidence: Line 2: `import scipy.stats as stats`. No reference to `stats.` appears anywhere in the file. All sampling uses np.random (np.random.uniform, np.random.normal). scipy is listed as a required dependency in requirements.txt, adding a heavy dependency for zero benefit in this module.
findings.intent_link: Clean dependency management for a scientific computing package.
findings.falsifier_note: src/parameter_sampler.py:2 is `import scipy.stats as stats`. Reading the full module (lines 1-162), no `stats.` reference appears; all sampling uses np.random.uniform/np.random.normal (lines 25, 41, 60-62, 83). The import is unused; scipy is a declared dependency (requirements.txt:4, setup.py:13).

findings.title: FORMAT_CSV constant is defined but save_residuals always returns False for CSV format
findings.evidence: residual_analysis.py:6: `FORMAT_CSV = 'csv'`. save_residuals() lines 154-177: the CSV branch prints 'Warning: CSV saving is not yet implemented' and `return False`. The constant is exported as part of the public API (used in save_residuals signature default), misleading callers into thinking CSV is a supported format option.
findings.intent_link: API contract: exported constants should represent supported functionality.
findings.falsifier_note: residual_analysis.py:6 defines FORMAT_CSV='csv'. save_residuals CSV branch (lines 154-177) prints 'CSV saving is not yet implemented' and returns False. Core finding confirmed. Minor inaccuracy in the parenthetical: the signature default is FORMAT_NPZ, not CSV (line 114: format=FORMAT_NPZ); FORMAT_CSV is a public module constant but not the default. This does not undermine the doc-drift finding.

findings.title: parameter_sampler.generate_pbh_sample does not sample PBH initial-position angles — encounter geometry is incomplete
findings.evidence: generate_pbh_sample() returns dicts with keys: 'mass_msun', 'impact_param_au', 'velocity_au_day', 't_encounter_years'. docs/pseudocode.md:201-228 specifies CreatePBHBody() requires: r0 (initial radial distance), theta0, phi0 (approach direction angles), alpha, beta (velocity orientation angles). None of these are sampled. Without initial position angles, the PBH position cannot be computed from encounter parameters (confirmed by the placeholder at simulation_runner.py:90-91).
findings.intent_link: Intent (1) and (3): physically meaningful N-body simulation of a PBH encounter requires fully specifying the encounter geometry in 3D; the sampler produces incomplete parameter sets.
findings.falsifier_note: generate_pbh_sample (parameter_sampler.py:85-120) builds dicts with exactly mass_msun, impact_param_au, velocity_au_day, t_encounter_years (lines 112-117). No sampling of r0/theta0/phi0/alpha/beta. docs/pseudocode.md:201-219 specifies CreatePBHBody requires r0, theta0, phi0, alpha, beta to set the PBH's initial position. The placeholder at simulation_runner.py:90-91 ('TODO: Calculate initial PBH position', dummy hardcoded position) confirms the geometry cannot be derived. Upheld.

findings.title: Nested multiprocessing: ensemble pool workers attempt to spawn a second Pool — AssertionError on every perturbed run
findings.evidence: run_ensemble (line 238) submits run_ensemble_member tasks to a multiprocessing.Pool; pool workers run as daemon processes. run_ensemble_member line 64 calls simulation_runner.run_parallel_simulations, which creates another multiprocessing.Pool at simulation_runner.py:228. Python raises 'AssertionError: daemonic processes are not allowed to have children' whenever a daemon process attempts to spawn a Pool. This makes every perturbed ensemble member fail immediately, rendering the entire ensemble pipeline non-functional.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to run large Monte Carlo ensemble simulations (millions of flyby samples); nested pool creation aborts every perturbed member before any N-body work begins.
findings.falsifier_note: run_ensemble (ensemble_runner.py:238) creates multiprocessing.Pool and maps run_ensemble_member; pool workers are daemonic by default. run_ensemble_member (line 64) calls simulation_runner.run_parallel_simulations, which at simulation_runner.py:228 unconditionally creates a second multiprocessing.Pool whenever pbh_params is not None. Ensemble always passes pbh_params (line 72), so the perturbed path always reaches line 228. Daemon processes cannot spawn children -> 'daemonic processes are not allowed to have children' AssertionError, caught by the member try/except and recorded as failed. Every member fails. Upheld.

findings.title: tqdm imported unconditionally at module level but absent from requirements.txt — ModuleNotFoundError on any fresh install
findings.evidence: Line 6: 'from tqdm import tqdm' is a bare unconditional top-level import with no try/except fallback. tqdm is absent from requirements.txt (which lists rebound, numpy, scipy, matplotlib, jupyter, emcee, dynesty) and from setup.py install_requires. Any environment built from the declared dependencies raises ModuleNotFoundError at import time, making ensemble_runner completely unavailable without an undocumented extra install step.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to run large ensemble simulations; the ensemble runner must be importable from a standard requirements-based install.
findings.falsifier_note: ensemble_runner.py:6 is `from tqdm import tqdm` — a bare top-level import (comment '# Optional' notwithstanding, there is no try/except). tqdm is used at line 241. requirements.txt lists only rebound/numpy/scipy/matplotlib/jupyter/emcee/dynesty (no tqdm); setup.py install_requires lists numpy/matplotlib/scipy/rebound/pytest/pytest-cov (no tqdm). A fresh install from declared deps raises ModuleNotFoundError on import of ensemble_runner. Upheld.

findings.title: PBH added to N-body simulation with full gravitational mass AND analytic impulse kick also applied — perturbation double-counted
findings.evidence: Lines 94-99 add the PBH as a live REBOUND particle with its full mass (pbh_mass). apply_analytic_kick (line 111) then integrates the full N-body system from t_start to t_ca — during which the PBH's real gravitational mass continuously attracts all bodies — and then applies the full analytic impulse delta_v at t_ca (which represents the total integrated effect of a complete flyby from −∞ to +∞). After the kick the PBH remains in the simulation with full mass through [t_ca, t_end]. The PBH perturbation is thus counted three times: N-body on approach, the full analytic kick, and N-body on departure. The impulse approximation requires that the single kick captures the entire effect; the PBH must not simultaneously exert live N-body forces.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to use the impulse approximation as a computational shortcut for PBH encounters, replicating Tran et al.; using both N-body and analytic kick on the same run is contradictory and physically wrong.
findings.falsifier_note: simulation_runner.py:94-99 adds the PBH as a live REBOUND particle with full pbh_mass. apply_analytic_kick (n_body_simulation.py:198-298) integrates the full system from t_start to t_ca via integrate_to_time (line 272) — during which the live PBH gravitationally attracts all bodies — then adds the full analytic delta_v to the target velocity (lines 291-293), where delta_v from calculate_velocity_kick is the total impulse of a complete flyby. After apply_analytic_kick returns, the main loop (simulation_runner.py:143-175) keeps integrating to t_end with the PBH still present at full mass. The PBH effect is thus counted three times: live N-body on approach, the full analytic kick, and live N-body on departure. The impulse approximation requires the PBH exert no live forces. Upheld.

findings.title: Perturbed simulation pre-encounter state (t_start) permanently lost when output arrays are reallocated after kick
findings.evidence: Lines 67-75 store the initial state at t=t_start into positions_out[0] and velocities_out[0] for len(masses) particles. When pbh_params is present, lines 126-127 reallocate positions_out and velocities_out to new zero arrays of shape (n_steps+1, len(masses)+1, 3), discarding the old arrays and their row-0 contents entirely. Lines 130-133 then overwrite times_out[0] with t_ca (the post-kick time) and fill the new positions_out[0] with the post-kick state at t_ca — not t_start. The perturbed trajectory output therefore begins at t_ca, not t_start. compute_residuals (residual_analysis.py:98) uses np.interp which silently clips to boundary values for query times before the perturbed array's first time point, making residuals for [t_start, t_ca] silently wrong.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to compute full-window orbital residuals between baseline and perturbed trajectories; losing the perturbed pre-encounter state corrupts the residual time series.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed at simulation_runner.py. Lines 67-75 fill positions_out[0]/velocities_out[0] with the t_start state for len(masses) particles. When pbh_params is truthy, lines 126-127 reassign positions_out/velocities_out to fresh np.zeros of shape (n_steps+1, len(masses)+1, 3), discarding row 0. Lines 130-133 set times_out[0]=sim_instance.get_simulation_time() and fill positions_out[0] with the current state. apply_analytic_kick (n_body_simulation.py:272) integrates the sim to t_apply (=t_ca) before returning, so the current time is t_ca, not t_start. Thus the perturbed output array begins at t_ca and the [t_start, t_ca] pre-encounter state is permanently lost; compute_residuals (residual_analysis.py:98) np.interp then clips for query times below t_ca.

findings.title: compute_residuals uses np.interp which silently clips to boundary values when time ranges do not fully overlap — produces wrong residuals without warning
findings.evidence: Lines 98-99: np.interp(residual_times, pert_times, pert_pos[:, particle_idx, dim]) with no range-overlap check. Per NumPy documentation np.interp extrapolates by returning the first or last value when query points fall outside the interpolation range. If base_times extends beyond pert_times (structurally guaranteed when the perturbed run starts at t_ca as described in the output-array reallocation bug), residuals for out-of-range steps use a constant boundary value, silently producing incorrect residuals. The only guard (line 86) checks for identical arrays, not range overlap.
findings.intent_link: The project intends residual computation to be the primary observable for PBH detection; silently wrong residuals directly corrupt detection rate estimates.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed at residual_analysis.py:98-99: np.interp(residual_times, pert_times, pert_pos[:,particle_idx,dim]) with no overlap/range check. np.interp returns the boundary (first/last) value for query points outside [pert_times[0], pert_times[-1]] rather than raising or NaN-ing. The only guard is line 86 (identical-array fast path), which does not check range coverage. residual_times=base_times (line 81), so when base_times extends below/beyond pert_times the residual is computed against a clipped constant — silently wrong.

findings.title: dt_rebound set to None when dt_years is falsy; integration loop unconditionally dereferences it causing TypeError
findings.evidence: Line 38: dt_rebound = dt_years * 2 * np.pi if dt_years else None uses a truthy check, equating dt_years=0.0 with None. If dt_rebound is None, line 144 computes next_time_rebound = min(sim_instance.sim.t + dt_rebound, target_rebound_time), raising TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'float' and 'NoneType'. Line 65 correctly handles the falsy case for n_steps but the loop body does not. The docstring (line 21) implies dt_years may be omitted for adaptive integrators, making this a reachable crash path.
findings.intent_link: Adaptive integrators (ias15) do not require a fixed timestep; callers following the docstring that omit or zero dt_years hit an unguarded crash.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed. Line 38: dt_rebound = dt_years * 2 * np.pi if dt_years else None — a truthy test that maps dt_years=0.0 to None. Line 65 separately falls back to n_steps=100 for falsy dt_years, so the while loop at line 143 (current_step < n_steps) is entered, and line 144 evaluates min(sim_instance.sim.t + dt_rebound, ...) which raises TypeError (float + NoneType) when dt_rebound is None. The error is caught by the broad except at line 180 (returns None,None,None), but the reachable defect path the finding describes is real and dt_years is documented (line 21) as only used 'if needed'.

findings.title: apply_analytic_kick docstring states simulation must be at t=0 but implementation uses current simulation time — misleading contract
findings.evidence: The method docstring (lines 201-208) reads 'Assumes the simulation is at the initial time (t=0) where initial positions/velocities are used for the kick calculation.' The implementation (line 215 comment) contradicts this: 'We calculate the kick based on the state at the *current* simulation time.' The code uses self.get_simulation_time() and self.sim.t — the current time, not necessarily zero. This misleads callers who may assume the method can only be called at t=0, preventing correct use in multi-phase pipelines where apply_analytic_kick might be called mid-simulation.
findings.intent_link: Accurate API documentation is essential for researchers composing multi-phase simulation pipelines involving pre-encounter integration followed by kick application.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed contradiction in n_body_simulation.py. Docstring lines 202-204: 'Assumes the simulation is at the initial time (t=0) where initial positions/velocities are used for the kick calculation.' Implementation lines 214-217 read initial_time_years = self.get_simulation_time() and comment 'We calculate the kick based on the state at the *current* simulation time.' Line 243 uses self.sim.t as the base for t_ca. The code uses current time, not t=0; the docstring is misleading. doc-drift/info confirmed.

findings.title: visualization.py and synthetic_data.py __main__ blocks use bare (non-relative) imports incompatible with package-mode execution
findings.evidence: visualization.py line 510 inside __main__: 'from ensemble_runner import calculate_detection_rates'. synthetic_data.py line 156 inside __main__ try block: 'import visualization'. Both are bare absolute imports. When these modules are imported as part of the src package (e.g., python -m src.visualization), the bare names are not resolvable via the package namespace; they require src/ to be on sys.path directly. In synthetic_data.py the ImportError is silently swallowed at line 174, skipping the comparison plot. In visualization.py the except ImportError at line 528 silently skips the binned rate example. This is the same class of defect as the already-flagged bare import in n_body_simulation.py:4 but at two additional uncovered locations.
findings.intent_link: The project intends src/ modules to work both as importable package members and as standalone demonstration scripts; bare imports in __main__ blocks break the package execution path silently.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed. visualization.py:510 inside __main__: 'from ensemble_runner import calculate_detection_rates' (bare, non-package import); its ImportError is silently caught at line 528 ('Skipping binned rate plot example'). synthetic_data.py:156 inside __main__ try block: 'import visualization' (bare); its ImportError is silently caught at line 174 ('Skipping comparison plot'). Both are bare absolute imports unresolvable under package-mode (python -m src.x) execution, matching the flagged n_body_simulation.py class of defect, at two additional locations with swallowed errors.

findings.title: calculate_detection_rates inflates total_completed denominator with unclassifiable members, biasing detection rate toward zero
findings.evidence: At line 367, `total_completed += 1` is executed unconditionally for every member with `status=='completed'`. At line 368, `is_detected(...)` is called, which returns `None` when stats are missing or invalid. Line 371 checks `if detected is None: continue`, skipping the member from `total_detected` AND from bin counts — but NOT reversing the `total_completed` increment already taken. The overall rate at line 394 is `total_detected / total_completed`, where `total_completed` includes unclassifiable members that contribute neither to the numerator nor to any bin. Separately, members with valid detection status whose mass falls outside `mass_bins` are counted in `total_completed` but not in any `totals_by_bin`, making the overall rate inconsistent with the sum of binned rates.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to estimate detection rates as a function of PBH mass from Monte Carlo ensembles. A biased denominator silently underestimates detection rates, corrupting the primary scientific output.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed at ensemble_runner.py. Line 367: total_completed += 1 runs unconditionally for every member with status=='completed'. Line 368 calls is_detected, which returns None when stats are missing/invalid (lines 299,303,309,313). Line 370-372: 'if detected is None: continue' skips the member from total_detected and from bin counts without decrementing total_completed. Line 394 computes overall_rate = total_detected/total_completed, so unclassifiable members inflate the denominator. Separately, members whose mass falls outside mass_bins (line 385 bound check 0<=bin_index<num_bins) are counted in total_completed but added to no totals_by_bin, making overall and summed-binned rates inconsistent.

findings.title: plot_residual_timeseries uses residuals-array-local indices to index a labels list documented as indexed by original particle indices, mislabeling every plotted line
findings.evidence: At line 127, `valid_indices` is built as `[idx for idx in particle_indices if 0 <= idx < n_particles_res]` — these are 0-based positions in the *residuals* array. At line 137, `plot_labels = [labels[idx] for idx in valid_indices]` indexes the caller-supplied `labels` list using those same residuals-array positions. The docstring (line 97-98) documents `labels` as 'corresponding to the *original* particle indices before potential selection via particle_indices.' When residuals represent a subset that does not start at particle 0 (e.g., Earth+Jupiter after excluding the Sun), `valid_indices` are residuals indices 0,1,... while the correct labels are at `labels[original_idx]`. The `__main__` example at line 443-451 demonstrates the bug: `res_p_eg` holds Earth and PlanetX residuals (original particles 1 and 2), `particle_indices=[0,1]`, `labels=['Sun','Earth','PlanetX']`; the function assigns labels 'Sun' and 'Earth' instead of 'Earth' and 'PlanetX'.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to generate publication-quality visualizations of orbital residuals. Systematically mislabeled residual time-series plots corrupt any analysis or figure derived from them.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed at visualization.py. Line 127 builds valid_indices = [idx for idx in particle_indices if 0 <= idx < n_particles_res] — values bounded by the residuals array size, i.e. residuals-array positions. Line 132 selects residuals[:, valid_indices, :] (residuals-local indexing) and line 137 reuses the same valid_indices to index the caller's labels list, which the docstring (lines 96-98) documents as indexed by *original* particle indices. The __main__ example (lines 442-451) passes res_p_eg (columns = original particles 1 and 2 per lines 431-437), particle_indices=[0,1], labels=['Sun','Earth','PlanetX'] (labels_eg), so plot_labels becomes [labels[0],labels[1]]=['Sun','Earth'] instead of the correct ['Earth','PlanetX']. Mislabeling confirmed.

findings.title: is_detected computes norm of per-dimension temporal peak values, overestimating true peak 3D displacement and producing false positive detections
findings.evidence: At line 316, `peak_residual_magnitude = np.linalg.norm(pos_peak_au[target_particle_idx, :])`. The array `pos_peak_au` is the output of `calculate_peak` (residual_analysis.py:293: `np.max(np.abs(data_residuals), axis=0)`), so each element is the temporal maximum for one dimension independently. These per-dimension maxima occur at different time steps. The Euclidean norm of `[max_x, max_y, max_z]` gives `sqrt(max_x^2 + max_y^2 + max_z^2)`, which is strictly >= the true peak 3D magnitude `max_t(sqrt(x(t)^2+y(t)^2+z(t)^2))` by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. The overestimate can cause the threshold comparison `peak_residual_magnitude > threshold_au` to return True (detected) when the actual peak 3D displacement never exceeded the threshold at any single moment.
findings.intent_link: The project intends to calculate detection rates from residual statistics. Overestimating peak displacement inflates the detection rate and invalidates rate estimates, undermining the core Monte Carlo analysis goal.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed at ensemble_runner.py:316: peak_residual_magnitude = np.linalg.norm(pos_peak_au[target_particle_idx, :]). pos_peak_au comes from calculate_peak (residual_analysis.py:293: np.max(np.abs(data_residuals), axis=0)), so its three entries are per-dimension temporal maxima taken independently (generally at different time steps). sqrt(max_x^2+max_y^2+max_z^2) >= max_t sqrt(x(t)^2+y(t)^2+z(t)^2) since each per-dim max bounds that dim's value at every t; the norm therefore overestimates the true peak 3D displacement and can make peak_residual_magnitude > threshold_au (line 318) when no instantaneous 3D displacement ever exceeded the threshold, yielding false-positive detections. (The 'Cauchy-Schwarz' attribution is loose, but the inequality direction is correct.)

findings.title: src/ directory has no __init__.py; namespace-package status prevents pip-installable package even if directory name or find_packages() call is corrected
findings.evidence: Glob for `**/__init__.py` returns only `tests/__init__.py`; there is no `src/__init__.py`. Python's `setuptools.find_packages()` identifies packages by the presence of `__init__.py`. Without it, `find_packages()` returns an empty list regardless of the `name=` or `where=` arguments, so `pip install .` installs nothing from `src/`. In Python 3, `src` is treated as a namespace package, which works for development (when `src/` is on sys.path) but breaks upon installation: installed namespace packages do not support relative imports (`from . import parameter_sampler`), which are used in ensemble_runner.py, simulation_runner.py, synthetic_data.py, and visualization.py via try/except blocks. The existing finding at setup.py:9 attributes the failure to a directory-name mismatch; the missing `__init__.py` is a separate, independent cause that would remain even if the directory were renamed correctly.
findings.intent_link: The project is packaged via setup.py and is intended to be importable as a module. Without __init__.py the package cannot be installed or used in relative-import mode by external consumers.
findings.falsifier_note: Confirmed by direct inspection. Glob for **/__init__.py returns only tests/__init__.py; no src/__init__.py exists. setup.py:9 calls find_packages() with no where= argument, so it scans the project root; without src/__init__.py, 'src' is not detected as a regular package and pip install . would install nothing from src/. Relative imports requiring package context are present: simulation_runner.py:4-5 (from . import n_body_simulation/analytic_impulse), ensemble_runner.py:10-13 (from . import parameter_sampler/...), synthetic_data.py:6 (from . import residual_analysis). This is a distinct, independent cause from a directory-name/find_packages mismatch. Minor inaccuracy in the finding's supporting detail: visualization.py does NOT use a try/except relative import; it uses an absolute 'from ensemble_runner import calculate_detection_rates' at line 510 inside __main__. This does not undermine the core design defect, which is upheld.

findings.title: run-task-master.bat hardcodes developer-specific username 'Shadow'
findings.evidence: Lines 7-8 embed 'C:\Users\Shadow\AppData\Roaming\npm' twice. Every developer whose Windows username is not 'Shadow' will get a path-not-found error on every Task Master command. The documented template in docs/task-master-windows-guide.md:68-72 correctly uses the portable %USERNAME% environment variable, confirming this is a developer-specific artifact that was inadvertently committed.
findings.intent_link: Breaks the TaskMaster collaboration workflow (onboarding-guide.md Section 1 'TaskMaster Setup') for all contributors except the original developer.
findings.falsifier_note:
Verified: run-task-master.bat:7 (`set PATH=%PATH%;C:\Program Files
odejs;C:\Users\Shadow\AppData\Roaming
pm`) and :8 (`C:\Users\Shadow\AppData\Roaming
pm	ask-master.cmd %*`) both hardcode username 'Shadow'. The portable template using %USERNAME% exists at docs/task-master-windows-guide.md:69-70 (finding cites 68-72, header inclusive). A non-'Shadow' user gets a path-not-found. Claim accurate.

findings.title: Three mutually inconsistent documented versions of run-task-master.bat
findings.evidence: onboarding-guide.md:113-115 shows '@echo off / npx task-master-ai %*' (portable, no path manipulation). task-master-windows-guide.md:68-72 shows a three-line file using '%USERNAME%' for portable npm paths. The committed run-task-master.bat uses a five-line file with hardcoded 'Shadow' username. None of the three versions matches any other.
findings.intent_link: New contributors following either documentation source will produce a file that differs from what is committed, undermining reproducibility of the development environment.
findings.falsifier_note: Three inconsistent versions confirmed: committed run-task-master.bat:1-8 (node-check + hardcoded 'Shadow' set PATH + task-master.cmd); onboarding-guide.md:113-114 (`@echo off` / `npx task-master-ai %*`); task-master-windows-guide.md:68-70 (`@echo off` / set PATH with %USERNAME% / task-master.cmd %USERNAME%). None matches another. Minor inaccuracy: finding calls the committed file 'five-line' but it is 8 content lines; does not affect the three-way mismatch claim.

findings.title: Invalid Claude model ID 'claude-3-5-sonnet-20240229' in all TaskMaster .env examples
findings.evidence: All three docs specify MODEL=claude-3-5-sonnet-20240229. Claude 3.5 Sonnet launched in June 2024 with id claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620; no Anthropic model carries the date suffix 20240229. task-master-windows-guide.md:319-322 additionally lists this invalid ID in a 'current valid models' enumeration alongside the valid claude-3-opus-20240229 and claude-3-haiku-20240307. Developers copying this value verbatim will receive 401/404 API errors from Anthropic.
findings.intent_link: Blocks TaskMaster AI features (task generation, expansion, complexity analysis) for every new contributor until they manually discover and fix the model ID.
findings.falsifier_note: Core defect verified: invalid ID claude-3-5-sonnet-20240229 (3.5-Sonnet never carried the 20240229 date; real IDs are -20240620/-20241022, while 20240229 belongs to claude-3-opus/claude-3-sonnet) appears in copyable .env examples at onboarding-guide.md:101 (finding cited 102, off-by-1) and task-master-windows-guide.md:52 (finding cited 50, off-by-2), and in the 'current valid models' list at task-master-windows-guide.md:320. HOWEVER the finding's claim that all three docs and specifically task-master-guide.md:59 specify this ID is REFUTED: line 59 shows `MODEL: ... (e.g., "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219")`, a valid model, not the invalid one. Underlying defect holds; the 'all three docs' / tm-guide:59 attribution is wrong.

findings.title: .gitignore excludes scripts/ directory, preventing PRD from being version-controlled
findings.evidence: Line 199 lists 'scripts/' as an ignored path. docs/task-master-guide.md:107 instructs users to run 'task-master parse-prd scripts/prd.txt'. The PRD file that seeds the entire task database cannot be committed to the repository; every developer must obtain or recreate it out-of-band.
findings.intent_link: Undermines the TaskMaster-driven workflow that the project explicitly adopts: without a committed PRD, reproducible task generation is impossible for new contributors.
findings.falsifier_note: Verified: .gitignore:199 is `scripts/`. task-master-guide.md:107 instructs `task-master parse-prd scripts/prd.txt`. No negation (!scripts/prd.txt) present. The PRD seed file is therefore excluded from version control. Claim accurate.

findings.title: .gitignore excludes .cursor directory, preventing sharing of Cursor workflow rules
findings.evidence: Line 201 ignores '.cursor'. docs/task-master-guide.md:101 states 'The task-master init command creates a .cursor/rules/dev_workflow.mdc file. Cursor automatically loads this file, providing the AI agent with context about Task Master commands and workflow.' With .cursor gitignored, this rules file is never committed and each developer must regenerate it individually.
findings.intent_link: Each new Cursor-based contributor loses the pre-configured AI workflow context that the guide describes as central to the development environment.
findings.falsifier_note: Verified: .gitignore:201 is `.cursor`. task-master-guide.md:101 states `task-master init` creates `.cursor/rules/dev_workflow.mdc`. With .cursor ignored the rules file is never committed. Claim accurate.

findings.title: rebound_readme.md Paper 9 arXiv link is truncated and broken
findings.evidence: Line 115 reads '<https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/>' with no abstract identifier appended. All other paper links in the same file include a full ADS identifier (e.g. 'abs/2012A%26A...537A.128R'). This link resolves to a generic ADS search page rather than the specific paper.
findings.intent_link: Breaks citation traceability for REBOUND Paper 9 (high-order symplectic integrators), which is relevant reference material for the N-body simulation pipeline.
findings.falsifier_note: Verified: rebound_readme.md:115 (Paper 9, Rein, Tamayo & Brown 2019) ends with `<https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/>` with no ADS identifier appended, whereas peers (e.g. :111 `abs/2018MNRAS.473.3351R`) carry full identifiers. Link is truncated/broken. Claim accurate.

findings.title: docs/onboarding-guide.md uses Windows-only .bat syntax throughout a cross-platform setup guide
findings.evidence: All TaskMaster commands in the guide use '.\run-task-master.bat' with a Windows backslash and .bat extension. The same guide includes Linux/macOS venv activation instructions (lines 129-131), establishing cross-platform intent. No Unix equivalent (run-task-master.sh or 'npx task-master-ai') is provided. On Linux/macOS, '.bat' files do not execute natively.
findings.intent_link: Linux/macOS contributors — the likely majority in an astronomy/astrophysics research context — cannot follow any TaskMaster step in the onboarding guide without ad-hoc adaptation.
findings.falsifier_note: Verified: onboarding-guide.md uses `.\run-task-master.bat` at 147,152,157,164,169,182,208,213,396,404,447 (finding's specific line list drifts by 1-2 on several but every cited region resolves to a .bat invocation). Cross-platform intent established by macOS/Linux venv activation at :131 (`source venv/bin/activate`). No run-task-master.sh exists (only run-task-master.bat present); no npx fallback given. .bat does not run natively on Linux/macOS. Claim accurate.

findings.title: docs/onboarding-guide.md clone+upstream setup creates redundant remotes without a fork
findings.evidence: Line 73-74 clones the main repo URL (setting origin to it). Lines 79-80 immediately add 'upstream' pointing to the identical URL. A fork-based workflow requires forking the repo first so origin points to the fork and upstream to the canonical source. Without forking, both remotes resolve to the same repository, making the upstream remote meaningless and misleading contributors about the intended contribution model.
findings.intent_link: Misleads contributors into a non-functional fork workflow; direct pushes to origin would go to the main repo, not a personal fork.
findings.falsifier_note: Verified: onboarding-guide.md:73 clones https://github.com/ImmortalDemonGod/PrimordialEncounters.git (origin) and :79 adds upstream pointing to the identical URL, with no fork step. Both remotes resolve to the same repo, making upstream redundant/misleading for a fork-based workflow. Claim accurate.

findings.title: docs/task-master-windows-guide.md file structure shows tasks.json at root with comment claiming it is inside tasks/
findings.evidence: Line 333 shows '├── tasks.json   # Task data (in tasks/ directory)' — the tree path places it at the project root but the inline comment claims it lives inside tasks/. The .gitignore ignores 'tasks.json' at root (line 197) and 'tasks/' directory (line 198), so both locations are excluded from version control regardless. The contradiction causes confusion about actual task data location.
findings.intent_link: Minor but confusing for contributors trying to understand where task state is persisted.
findings.falsifier_note: Content verified: task-master-windows-guide.md shows `tasks.json   # Task data (in tasks/ directory)` placed at project-root level in the tree while the inline comment claims it lives inside tasks/ — a contradiction. Both .gitignore:197 (tasks.json) and :198 (tasks/) exclude either location. Minor inaccuracy: the line is 331, not the cited 333 (off-by-2). Substantive claim holds.

refuted_or_unverified.title: run_simulation does not pass exact_finish_time=1, causing WHFast/Mercurius runs to overshoot target end time
refuted_or_unverified.evidence: run_simulation line 145 calls self.sim.integrate(target_rebound_time) without exact_finish_time=1. The sibling method integrate_to_time line 169 explicitly passes exact_finish_time=1 with the comment 'for precision'. For WHFast and Mercurius (the default integrators, line 43) REBOUND advances one full timestep past target_rebound_time when exact_finish_time is not set, producing a final time that overshoots the requested end. This inconsistency means run_simulation and integrate_to_time produce different final times for the same target, breaking time-aligned comparisons between baseline and perturbed results.
refuted_or_unverified.intent_link: Accurate end-time control is required for computing residuals at matching time points between baseline and perturbed simulations.
refuted_or_unverified.falsifier_note: The behavioral claim is false: REBOUND's Python API Simulation.integrate(self, tmax, exact_finish_time=1) defaults exact_finish_time to 1. run_simulation (n_body_simulation.py:145) calling self.sim.integrate(target_rebound_time) without the kwarg therefore uses exact_finish_time=1 — identical to integrate_to_time's explicit pass at line 169. Both finish exactly at the target; there is no one-timestep overshoot and no divergence in final times for the same target. The cited code difference exists but does not produce the asserted effect.

refuted_or_unverified.title: docs/task-master-guide.md lists 'npx task-master-init' as local-install init command instead of 'npx task-master-ai init'
refuted_or_unverified.evidence: Lines 46-48 state 'If installed locally: npx task-master-init'. The npm package name is 'task-master-ai' (lines 21-22 in the same file). 'npx task-master-init' would attempt to execute a separate package called 'task-master-init' which does not exist on npm. The global form on line 40 correctly uses 'task-master init' (space-separated subcommand). The local form should be 'npx task-master-ai init'.
refuted_or_unverified.intent_link: Local-installation users following this doc will get an npm package-not-found error when trying to initialize Task Master.
